<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

<collaboration_mode># Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.
</collaboration_mode>

<skills_instructions>
## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- imagegen: Generate or edit raster images when the task benefits from AI-created bitmap visuals such as photos, illustrations, textures, sprites, mockups, or transparent-background cutouts. Use when Codex should create a brand-new image, transform an existing image, or derive visual variants from references, and the output should be a bitmap asset rather than repo-native code or vector. Do not use when the task is better handled by editing existing SVG/vector/code-native assets, extending an established icon or logo system, or building the visual directly in HTML/CSS/canvas. (file: C:/Users/Genet Abebe/.codex/skills/.system/imagegen/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations, help choosing the latest model for a use case, or explicit GPT-5.4 upgrade and prompt-upgrade guidance; prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools, use bundled references only as helper context, and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: C:/Users/Genet Abebe/.codex/skills/.system/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- plugin-creator: Create and scaffold plugin directories for Codex with a required `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`, optional plugin folders/files, and baseline placeholders you can edit before publishing or testing. Use when Codex needs to create a new local plugin, add optional plugin structure, or generate or update repo-root `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json` entries for plugin ordering and availability metadata. (file: C:/Users/Genet Abebe/.codex/skills/.system/plugin-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: C:/Users/Genet Abebe/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: C:/Users/Genet Abebe/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.
</skills_instructions>

You are the reviewer responsible for Delivery Acceptance and Project Architecture Audit.

Work only within the current working directory.

This is a static-only audit.
Do not start the project.
Do not run Docker.
Do not run tests automatically.
Do not modify code.
Do not infer runtime success from documentation alone.

Your job is to find as many material issues as possible, especially Blocker / High severity issues, while keeping all conclusions evidence-based and traceable.

[Business / Task Prompt]
A SentinelFit Operations platform capable of managing offline member access, coaching content performance, and full-lifecycle face data governance for a gym or training studio environment. The Vue.js web UI (English) starts with a local account sign-in that supports quick re-entry on the same workstation (optional 4–6 digit PIN after a successful password login) and optional phone number binding strictly as a contact attribute with no SMS or verification; roles are Member (user), Coach, and Administrator with a clear hierarchy and role-based navigation. Members can enroll with a coach, consent to face enrollment, and complete face capture at a front desk station using a connected camera or image import, receiving immediate on-screen feedback for quality gates and duplication warnings. Coaches publish training tips and studio announcements, then review in-app analytics such as content views by source channel (e.g., lobby kiosk, front-desk terminal), top-performing posts, and onsite search term trends; they can filter by date range (MM/DD/YYYY) and location, and drill into linked charts without leaving the page. Administrators use a configurable dashboard builder with drag-and-drop charts and reusable report templates, exportable to CSV/Excel/PDF, and can set scheduled generation (for example, every Monday at 6:00 AM) with “subscriptions” delivered as an in-app report inbox and a downloadable file stored on a designated shared folder path, keeping the workflow fully offline.


[Acceptance / Scoring Criteria (the only authority)]
{
1. Hard Gates

1.1 Documentation and static verifiability
- Whether clear startup / run / test / configuration instructions are provided
- Whether the documented entry points, configuration, and project structure appear statically consistent
- Whether the delivery provides enough static evidence for a human reviewer to attempt verification without first rewriting core code

1.2 Whether the delivered project materially deviates from the Prompt
- Whether the implementation is centered on the business goal or usage scenario described in the Prompt
- Whether there are major parts of the implementation that are only loosely related, or unrelated, to the Prompt
- Whether the project replaces, weakens, or ignores the core problem definition in the Prompt without justification

2. Delivery Completeness

2.1 Whether the delivered project fully covers the core requirements explicitly stated in the Prompt
- Whether all explicitly stated core functional requirements in the Prompt are implemented

2.2 Whether the delivered project represents a basic end-to-end deliverable from 0 to 1, rather than a partial feature, illustrative implementation, or code fragment
- Whether mock / hardcoded behavior is used in place of real logic without explanation
- Whether the project includes a complete project structure rather than scattered code or a single-file example
- Whether basic project documentation is provided, such as a README or equivalent

3. Engineering and Architecture Quality

3.1 Whether the project adopts a reasonable engineering structure and module decomposition for the scale of the problem
- Whether the project structure is clear and module responsibilities are reasonably defined
- Whether the project contains redundant or unnecessary files
- Whether the implementation is excessively piled into a single file

3.2 Whether the project shows basic maintainability and extensibility, rather than being a temporary or stacked implementation
- Whether there are obvious signs of chaotic structure or tight coupling
- Whether the core logic leaves room for extension rather than being completely hard-coded

4. Engineering Details and Professionalism

4.1 Whether the engineering details and overall shape reflect professional software practice, including but not limited to error handling, logging, validation, and API design
- Whether error handling is basically reliable and user-friendly
- Whether logs support troubleshooting rather than being random print statements or completely absent
- Whether necessary validation is present for key inputs and boundary conditions

4.2 Whether the project is organized like a real product or service, rather than remaining at the level of an example or demo
- Whether the overall deliverable resembles a real application instead of a teaching sample or demonstration-only project

5. Prompt Understanding and Requirement Fit

5.1 Whether the project accurately understands and responds to the business goal, usage scenario, and implicit constraints described in the Prompt, rather than merely implementing surface-level technical features
- Whether the core business objective in the Prompt is implemented correctly
- Whether there are obvious misunderstandings of the requirement semantics or deviations from the actual problem
- Whether key constraints in the Prompt are changed or ignored without explanation

6. Aesthetics (frontend-only / full-stack tasks only)

6.1 Whether the visual and interaction design fits the scenario and demonstrates reasonable visual quality
- Whether different functional areas of the page are visually distinguishable through background, spacing, separation, or hierarchy
- Whether the overall layout is reasonable, and whether alignment, spacing, and proportions are broadly consistent
- Whether UI elements, including text, images, and icons, render and display correctly
- Whether visual elements are consistent with the page theme and textual content, and whether there are obvious mismatches between images, illustrations, decorative elements, and the actual content
- Whether basic interaction feedback is provided, such as hover states, click states, or transitions, so users can understand the current interaction state
- Whether fonts, font sizes, colors, and icon styles are generally consistent, without obvious visual inconsistency or mixed design language
}

====================
Hard Rules (must follow)

1) Static-only audit boundary
- Perform static analysis only.
- Do not run the project, tests, Docker, or external services.
- Do not claim that a flow works at runtime unless this is directly proven by static evidence such as implementation completeness plus tests that clearly cover the flow.
- Any conclusion that depends on real runtime behavior, environment setup, network access, container orchestration, browser interaction, timing, or external integrations must be marked as:
  - Cannot Confirm Statistically
  - or Manual Verification Required

2) Prompt-to-code alignment is mandatory
- Prompt is the core constraint for the whole audit.
- You must extract the core business goal, main flows, explicit requirements, and important implicit constraints from the Prompt.
- You must compare those requirement points against the actual code, structure, interfaces, data model, tests, and documentation.
- Do not review the repository as a generic codebase only; always judge it against the Prompt.

3) Risk-first scan order is allowed and recommended
- You do not need to review in the same order as the six acceptance sections.
- You may scan in any order that improves speed and accuracy, but the final report must still be organized by the six major acceptance sections.
- Recommended scan priority:
  1. README / docs / config examples / manifests / env examples
  2. Entry points and route registration
  3. Authentication / session / token / middleware / permission guards
  4. Core business modules, services, data models, persistence layer
  5. Admin / internal / debug endpoints
  6. Test files and test configuration
  7. Frontend UI structure and visual consistency if applicable

4) Do not omit material findings, but avoid wasteful repetition
- The final report must cover every major acceptance section and every secondary requirement under it.
- If an item is not applicable, mark it as Not Applicable and explain the boundary briefly.
- If an item cannot be proven statically, mark it as Cannot Confirm Statistically and explain why.
- Merge repeated symptoms into root-cause findings where appropriate.
- Do not expand low-value duplicate issues across many files if they are caused by the same root cause.

5) Evidence must be traceable
- Every key conclusion must include precise evidence with file path + line number, such as `README.md:10` or `app/main.py:42`.
- Do not make unsupported judgments.
- Strong conclusions such as Pass / Fail / Blocker / High must not be based on vague impressions.
- Missing in the reviewed scope does not automatically mean missing in the repository; only conclude Missing when the reviewed evidence is sufficient to support that conclusion. Otherwise use Cannot Confirm Statistically.

6) Every judgment must be justified
- For every judgment such as Pass / Partial Pass / Fail / Not Applicable / Cannot Confirm Statistically, explain the reasoning briefly and concretely.
- The basis may come from:
  - alignment against the Prompt
  - alignment against the acceptance criteria
  - alignment against common engineering / architectural practice
  - code-level implementation evidence
  - static test evidence
  - documentation-to-code consistency evidence

7) Security review has priority
- Pay special attention to authentication, authorization, privilege boundaries, and data isolation.
- You must explicitly examine and assess, with evidence:
  - authentication entry points
  - route-level authorization
  - object-level authorization
  - function-level authorization
  - tenant / user data isolation
  - admin / internal / debug endpoint protection
- If a likely security risk is suggested but not fully provable statically, mark it as Suspected Risk or Cannot Confirm Statistically rather than overstating it.

8) Mock / stub / fake handling
- Mock / stub / fake behavior is not a defect by itself unless the Prompt or documentation explicitly requires real third-party integration or real production behavior.
- You must still explain:
  - how the mock behavior is implemented
  - under what conditions it is enabled
  - whether there is a risk of accidental production use
  - whether validation or safety checks can be bypassed because of it

9) Tests and logging are mandatory review dimensions
- You must statically assess unit tests, API / integration tests, and logging.
- Do not run them.
- Assess:
  - whether they exist
  - what they appear to cover
  - whether they cover core flows and important failure paths
  - whether logging categories are meaningful
  - whether there is a sensitive-data leakage risk in logs or responses

10) Static audit of test coverage is mandatory, but keep it risk-focused
- First extract the core business requirements and major risk points from the Prompt and implementation.
- Then map the most important requirement / risk points to the existing tests.
- Focus especially on:
  - core happy path
  - input validation failures
  - unauthenticated 401
  - unauthorized 403
  - not found 404
  - conflict / duplicate submission where relevant
  - object-level authorization
  - tenant / user isolation
  - pagination / sorting / filtering where relevant
  - empty data / extreme values / time fields / concurrency / repeated requests / rollback where relevant
  - sensitive log exposure
- Build coverage mapping for high-risk and core requirement areas first.
- You do not need to produce a bloated exhaustive matrix for every trivial requirement if the same root cause or same coverage gap already explains the risk.
- Still clearly state which important risks are sufficiently covered, insufficiently covered, missing, not applicable, or cannot confirm.

11) Severity rating
- Every issue must be severity-rated as:
  - Blocker
  - High
  - Medium
  - Low
- Prioritize reporting independent root-cause issues over repeated surface symptoms.
- For each issue include:
  - severity
  - conclusion
  - evidence
  - impact
  - minimum actionable fix
  - minimal verification path or manual verification suggestion if useful

12) No code modification
- This task is review only.
- Do not modify code to make the project appear to pass.
- If changes would be required, record them under Issues / Suggestions only.

====================
Output Requirements

Produce the final audit in a concise but complete report and write the consolidated report to `./.tmp/**.md`.

The final report must be organized by the six major acceptance sections in order, even if your scan order was different.

Use this structure:

1. Verdict
- Overall conclusion: Pass / Partial Pass / Fail / Cannot Confirm Statistically

2. Scope and Static Verification Boundary
- What was reviewed
- What was not reviewed
- What was intentionally not executed
- Which claims require manual verification

3. Repository / Requirement Mapping Summary
- Summarize the Prompt’s core business goal, core flows, and major constraints
- Summarize the main implementation areas you mapped against those requirements
- This section should be short and functional, not verbose

4. Section-by-section Review

For each major section and each secondary item under it, provide:
- Conclusion: Pass / Partial Pass / Fail / Not Applicable / Cannot Confirm Statistically
- Rationale: brief reasoning tied to Prompt and code
- Evidence: `file:line`
- Manual verification note only if static proof is insufficient and human follow-up is needed

5. Issues / Suggestions (Severity-Rated)

List all material issues found.
For each issue provide:
- Severity
- Title
- Conclusion
- Evidence (`file:line`)
- Impact
- Minimum actionable fix

Rules for issue listing:
- Report Blocker / High issues first
- Merge duplicate manifestations caused by the same root cause
- Do not inflate severity for style-only issues
- Do not treat lack of runtime execution as a defect
- Do not treat acceptable mocks as defects unless they violate the Prompt

6. Security Review Summary

Provide explicit conclusions, with evidence, for:
- authentication entry points
- route-level authorization
- object-level authorization
- function-level authorization
- tenant / user isolation
- admin / internal / debug protection

For each one, use:
- Pass / Partial Pass / Fail / Cannot Confirm Statistically
- plus brief evidence and reasoning

7. Tests and Logging Review

Provide separate conclusions for:
- Unit tests
- API / integration tests
- Logging categories / observability
- Sensitive-data leakage risk in logs / responses

8. Test Coverage Assessment (Static Audit)

This section is mandatory.

It must contain:

8.1 Test Overview
- Whether unit tests and API / integration tests exist
- Test framework(s)
- Test entry points
- Whether documentation provides test commands
- Evidence (`file:line`)

8.2 Coverage Mapping Table
For each core requirement or high-risk point reviewed, provide:
- Requirement / Risk Point
- Mapped Test Case(s) (`file:line`)
- Key Assertion / Fixture / Mock (`file:line`)
- Coverage Assessment: sufficient / basically covered / insufficient / missing / not applicable / cannot confirm
- Gap
- Minimum Test Addition

8.3 Security Coverage Audit
Provide coverage conclusions for:
- authentication
- route authorization
- object-level authorization
- tenant / data isolation
- admin / internal protection
For each, explain whether tests meaningfully cover the risk or whether severe defects could still remain undetected.

8.4 Final Coverage Judgment
The conclusion must be exactly one of:
- Pass
- Partial Pass
- Fail
- Cannot Confirm

Then explain the boundary clearly:
- which major risks are covered
- which uncovered risks mean the tests could still pass while severe defects remain

9. Final Notes
- Keep the report precise, evidence-based, and non-repetitive.
- Do not pad the report with generic advice.
- Do not overstate what static analysis can prove.

====================
Review Discipline

Before finalizing any strong conclusion, ask yourself:
- Is this directly supported by file:line evidence?
- Is this a static fact, or am I implying runtime behavior?
- Am I reporting a root cause, or only repeating symptoms?
- Have I judged this against the Prompt rather than generic preferences?
- If uncertain, should this be Cannot Confirm Statistically instead?

Your priority is:
1. Find real material defects
2. Keep conclusions evidence-based
3. Reduce hallucination
4. Preserve final completeness
5. Avoid unnecessary repetition

I’m treating this as a static delivery and architecture audit against the SentinelFit prompt, and I’ll start by mapping the repo structure, docs, and entry points before drilling into auth, RBAC, core modules, tests, and logging.

   1: # SentinelFit Operations Platform
   2: 
   3: SentinelFit is a fully on-prem operations platform for gyms and training studios. This implementation delivers the planned vertical slices across authentication, member enrollment, biometric governance, coaching content, analytics, dashboarding, scheduled reporting, observability, and backup/recovery using a Docker-first runtime.
   4: 
   5: The implementation benchmark used for this repository is [temp/final_plan_of_action.md](/E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/temp/final_plan_of_action.md).
   6: 
   7: ## Stack
   8: 
   9: - Frontend: Vue 3, TypeScript, Pinia, Tailwind CSS, TensorFlow.js, Chart.js
  10: - Backend: Express, TypeScript, Node.js, node-cron
  11: - Database: MySQL 8
  12: - Runtime: Docker Compose, fully offline/on-prem, no external API dependencies
  13: 
  14: ## Implemented modules
  15: 
  16: - Docker-first runtime with frontend, backend, primary MySQL, and standby MySQL
  17: - Password login, server-bound same-workstation PIN re-entry, warm-lock after inactivity, session expiry, and account-wide lockout handling
  18: - Signed request integrity with HMAC, nonce, and timestamp validation
  19: - Inclusive RBAC for Member, Coach, and Administrator
  20: - Member enrollment, coach assignment, masked phone handling, administrator/coach consent updates, member self-service face consent, and operator-entered member passwords with no shared UI default
  21: - Face enrollment with camera capture or image import, encrypted biometric artifacts, client-side quality guidance, pre-submit duplicate warnings, server-issued timed liveness challenges, server-derived face/liveness validation, dedup, versioning, deactivation, and immutable biometric audit records
  22: - Coach content publishing and analytics by station/location/date with MM/DD/YYYY filters, inline chart drill-down, and real view/search event capture
  23: - Admin dashboard layout persistence, configurable widget palette, and reusable report templates
  24: - Scheduled/offline reporting with subscriptions, inbox delivery, actionable downloads, and shared-folder output
  25: - Admin console for logs, alerts, latency/error metrics, backup, and dry-run restore
  26: - Encrypted local key vault with key version metadata and rotation support
  27: - Unit, integration-style, and Playwright end-to-end verification
  28: 
  29: ## Repository layout
  30: 
  31: - [frontend](/E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/frontend): Vue application
  32: - [backend](/E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/backend): Express API and background services
  33: - [tests/e2e](/E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/tests/e2e): Playwright end-to-end tests
  34: - [temp](/E:/Hale/Coding/Eaglepoint/Task-75/repo/temp): planning notes and implementation benchmark
  35: 
  36: ## Prerequisites
  37: 
  38: - Docker Desktop with Compose
  39: - Node.js 20+
  40: - npm 10+
  41: - Bash-compatible shell for `run_tests.sh`
  42: 
  43: ## Configuration
  44: 
  45: 1. Run with defaults directly via `docker compose up --build` for local/offline bootstrap on a fresh machine.
  46: 2. Optionally provide a `.env` file to override defaults for ports, credentials, shared-folder paths, or data directories.
  47: 
  48: Important environment variables:
  49: 
  50: - `MYSQL_DATABASE`
  51: - `MYSQL_USER`
  52: - `MYSQL_PASSWORD`
  53: - `MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD`
  54: - `MYSQL_PORT`
  55: - `MYSQL_PORT_BIND`
  56: - `MYSQL_HOST`
  57: - `MYSQL_STANDBY_HOST`
  58: - `MYSQL_STANDBY_PORT`
  59: - `MYSQL_STANDBY_PORT_BIND`
  60: - `BACKEND_PORT`
  61: - `BACKEND_NODE_ENV`
  62: - `BACKEND_ALLOWED_ORIGINS`
  63: - `BACKEND_DATA_DIR`
  64: - `BACKEND_REPORTS_SHARED_PATH`
  65: - `REPORTS_SHARED_HOST_PATH`
  66: - `BACKEND_IP_ALLOWLIST`
  67: - `BACKEND_API_RATE_LIMIT_PER_MINUTE`
  68: - `BACKEND_DEMO_SEED_USERS`
  69: - `BACKEND_KEY_VAULT_MASTER_KEY`
  70: - `FRONTEND_PORT`
  71: - `VITE_API_BASE_URL`
  72: 
  73: The default configuration allows both `localhost` and `127.0.0.1` frontend origins so browser verification remains stable in local and Docker-driven access paths.
  74: The shipped allowlist defaults cover local and private-LAN ranges so the offline deployment path does not start in an allow-all state.
  75: The Docker stack now binds report delivery to `REPORTS_SHARED_HOST_PATH` on the host so exported files are directly visible outside containers.
  76: 
  77: ## Run with Docker
  78: 
  79: The canonical startup command is:
  80: 
  81: ```bash
  82: docker compose up --build
  83: ```
  84: 
  85: Services:
  86: 
  87: - Frontend: [http://localhost:5173](http://localhost:5173)
  88: - Backend live health: [http://localhost:3000/health/live](http://localhost:3000/health/live)
  89: - Backend readiness: [http://localhost:3000/health/ready](http://localhost:3000/health/ready)
  90: - Primary MySQL: `localhost:3306`
  91: - Standby MySQL: `localhost:3307`
  92: 
  93: Shared report delivery:
  94: 
  95: - Host path: `${REPORTS_SHARED_HOST_PATH}` (default `./shared-reports`)
  96: - Container path: `${BACKEND_REPORTS_SHARED_PATH}` (default `/shared/reports`)
  97: - For Windows UNC/SMB shares, set `REPORTS_SHARED_HOST_PATH` to an absolute host-mounted path available to Docker Desktop.
  98: 
  99: ## Quick start (new machine)
 100: 
 101: 1. Install Docker Desktop (or Docker Engine + Compose plugin) and start Docker.
 102: 2. Open this repository directory.
 103: 3. Run:
 104: 
 105: ```bash
 106: docker compose up --build
 107: ```
 108: 
 109: 4. Wait until services are healthy, then open [http://localhost:5173](http://localhost:5173).
 110: 5. If `BACKEND_DEMO_SEED_USERS=false` (default), complete the one-time "First administrator setup".
 111: 6. To stop:
 112: 
 113: ```bash
 114: docker compose down
 115: ```
 116: 
 117: Notes:
 118: - No `.env` file is required for local startup; Compose defaults are provided.
 119: - Ensure ports `5173`, `3000`, `3306`, and `3307` are free on the host.
 120: 
 121: ## Troubleshooting
 122: 
 123: - Docker daemon not running:
 124:   Start Docker Desktop and retry `docker compose up --build`.
 125: 
 126: - Port already in use:
 127:   Find and stop conflicting processes, or override bind ports in `.env` (for example `FRONTEND_PORT`, `BACKEND_PORT`, `MYSQL_PORT_BIND`, `MYSQL_STANDBY_PORT_BIND`).
 128: 
 129: - Backend cannot connect to MySQL after config changes:
 130:   Recreate clean volumes:
 131:   ```bash
 132:   docker compose down --volumes --remove-orphans
 133:   docker compose up --build
 134:   ```
 135: 
 136: - Health endpoint not ready:
 137:   Check logs:
 138:   ```bash
 139:   docker compose logs backend
 140:   docker compose logs mysql
 141:   ```
 142:   Backend readiness endpoint: [http://localhost:3000/health/ready](http://localhost:3000/health/ready)
 143: 
 144: - UI loads but login/bootstrap fails:
 145:   Confirm backend is reachable at [http://localhost:3000/health/live](http://localhost:3000/health/live) and that station token input is set in the UI.
 146: 
 147: ## Demo seed accounts
 148: 
 149: Demo users are only created when `BACKEND_DEMO_SEED_USERS=true`.
 150: 
 151: - `admin` / `Admin12345!X`
 152: - `coach` / `Coach12345!X`
 153: - `member` / `Member12345!X`
 154: 
 155: The default non-demo runtime leaves seed users disabled so a clean deployment does not start with publicly documented credentials.
 156: 
 157: ## First-boot administrator setup
 158: 
 159: When `BACKEND_DEMO_SEED_USERS=false`, SentinelFit presents a one-time administrator bootstrap flow instead of relying on pre-seeded credentials.
 160: 
 161: 1. Start the stack with `docker compose up --build`
 162: 2. Open [http://localhost:5173](http://localhost:5173)
 163: 3. Complete the "First administrator setup" form
 164: 4. The created administrator is signed in immediately and the bootstrap path is closed once an administrator exists
 165: 
 166: ## Testing
 167: 
 168: The canonical unified test entrypoint is:
 169: 
 170: ```bash
 171: ./run_tests.sh
 172: ```
 173: 
 174: `run_tests.sh` performs:
 175: 
 176: - root dependency install
 177: - backend dependency install and coverage-checked tests
 178: - frontend dependency install and coverage-checked tests
 179: - Playwright browser install
 180: - `docker compose up --build -d`
 181: - readiness wait for frontend and backend
 182: - Playwright end-to-end tests
 183: - clean-install Docker restart with demo seeds disabled
 184: - first-boot Playwright bootstrap verification
 185: 
 186: Local verification commands are still useful, but Dockerized behavior is authoritative.
 187: Before running them directly, install package-local dependencies once:
 188: 
 189: ```bash
 190: cd backend && npm ci
 191: cd ../frontend && npm ci
 192: cd ..
 193: ```
 194: 
 195: Then run:
 196: 
 197: ```bash
 198: cd backend && npm test
 199: cd backend && npm run typecheck
 200: cd frontend && npm test
 201: cd frontend && npm run typecheck
 202: cd frontend && npm run build
 203: cd frontend && npm run preview
 204: npm run test:e2e
 205: ```
 206: 
 207: `npm run test:e2e` assumes frontend and backend services are already running and reachable on `127.0.0.1:5173` and `127.0.0.1:3000`. For a self-contained run, use `./run_tests.sh` (Docker canonical path).
 208: Local non-Docker checks validate source-level behavior and contracts, but deployment/runtime readiness is only guaranteed by the Docker-first path.
 209: 
 210: ### Frontend-only non-Docker review recipe
 211: 
 212: Use this path when Docker is unavailable and you only need frontend implementation validation:
 213: 
 214: ```bash
 215: cd frontend
 216: npm ci
 217: npm run typecheck
 218: npm test
 219: npm run build
 220: npm run dev
 221: ```
 222: 
 223: Coverage from this path:
 224: - Component/composable/store behavior and UI-level validation logic
 225: - Type safety and production frontend build output
 226: 
 227: Not covered by this path:
 228: - Backend/MySQL runtime integration
 229: - Playwright E2E against live backend services
 230: - Docker deployment readiness (`docker compose up --build`, `./run_tests.sh`)
 231: 
 232: ## Operational notes
 233: 
 234: - Sensitive fields are encrypted with AES-256 via a local key vault whose key material is envelope-encrypted at rest with `BACKEND_KEY_VAULT_MASTER_KEY`.
 235: - Key metadata is stored in MySQL and historical keys remain available for decrypt-on-read.
 236: - `KEY_VAULT_MASTER_KEY` defaults to `AUTO_GENERATE`, which creates a per-environment master key on first boot and stores it under the backend data directory.
 237: - `MYSQL_USER`, `MYSQL_PASSWORD`, and `MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD` have local bootstrap defaults for one-command startup; override them for production/shared deployments.
 238: - Persisted session secrets are encrypted and persisted session identifiers are stored as non-reusable hashes.
 239: - Biometric audit records are append-only and protected by MySQL triggers.
 240: - Stored biometric artifacts are encrypted before being written to disk.
 241: - Timed liveness challenges require the prompted head-turn capture to occur within the enforced server-side window.
 242: - Final face-in-frame and liveness acceptance are derived from image content on the server; client landmark clicks are guidance-only and never the source of truth.
 243: - The current biometric dedup/liveness layer uses lightweight local image heuristics suitable for offline baseline validation; high-assurance deployments should replace this with a stronger local face pipeline.
 244: - Face duplicate checks can be previewed before final enrollment submission so operators see warnings during capture review, not only after commit.
 245: - Preview-only dedup checks do not persist orphaned biometric artifacts to disk.
 246: - PIN re-entry is only valid when an active warm session already exists and the browser presents the server-issued workstation binding cookie; the station token remains attribution metadata, not the security proof.
 247: - PIN re-entry shares the sign-in rate limit path and lockout policy to reduce brute-force exposure.
 248: - Request-signing policy:
 249:   - Signed (`x-sf-timestamp` + `x-sf-nonce` + `x-sf-signature`) is required for all authenticated business routes under `/api/self`, `/api/members`, `/api/faces`, `/api/content`, `/api/dashboards`, `/api/reports`, and `/api/admin`.
 250:   - Unsigned exceptions are limited to pre-auth/session-establishment flows: `/api/auth/bootstrap/status`, `/api/auth/bootstrap/admin`, `/api/auth/login`, `/api/auth/pin/reenter`, and `/api/auth/session`.
 251:   - Compensating controls for unsigned exceptions include IP allowlist enforcement, auth-route per-IP throttling, credential lockout policy, and workstation-binding cookie validation on `/api/auth/session`.
 252:   - Replay protection for signed routes uses nonce single-use records stored in MySQL with TTL, so replay history survives process restarts and multi-instance routing.
 253: - Session-state restoration uses the server session endpoint and keeps the signing secret in memory only, while warm-lock resumes require PIN re-entry.
 254: - Coach access scope is driven by explicit `coach_location_assignments` records rather than inferred from historical member/content data.
 255: - Analytics filters are validated server-side before query execution, including malformed date-range rejection.
 256: - Report exports are written to both a local backend reports directory and the configured shared-folder path.
 257: - Scheduled reports persist their configured export format (`CSV`/`Excel`/`PDF`) and cron runs execute with that same format.
 258: - Scheduled reports can target multiple subscribers, and inbox items expose direct download actions.
 259: - Subscriber inbox access is available to authenticated recipients while schedule/template authoring remains administrator-only.
 260: - Generated reports use the requesting administrator or schedule owner dashboard layout rather than a fixed administrator snapshot.
 261: - The Reports surface loads template state directly, so generation and scheduling do not depend on visiting Dashboards first.
 262: - Shared-folder delivery failures raise `storage_sync_error` alerts in the admin console.
 263: - Admin metrics surface uptime, request latency, server error rate, and recent backup/report timings.
 264: - Startup retries database initialization so `docker compose up` is resilient to MySQL readiness races.
 265: - Nightly encrypted backups and dry-run restore workflows target the standby MySQL instance.
 266: - The local key vault attempts to harden its file permissions on write; operators should still apply OS-level restrictions to the backend data directory.
 267: 
 268: ## Verification checklist
 269: 
 270: - `docker compose up --build` starts all services without manual intervention.

   1: # SentinelFit API Spec
   2: 
   3: ## Base
   4: - Base URL: `http://<host>:3000`
   5: - Default local URLs:
   6:   - Frontend: `http://127.0.0.1:5173`
   7:   - Backend: `http://127.0.0.1:3000`
   8: 
   9: ## Envelope
  10: 
  11: ### Success
  12: ```json
  13: {
  14:   "ok": true,
  15:   "data": {}
  16: }
  17: ```
  18: 
  19: ### Error
  20: ```json
  21: {
  22:   "ok": false,
  23:   "error": {
  24:     "code": "string_code",
  25:     "message": "Human-readable message",
  26:     "details": null
  27:   }
  28: }
  29: ```
  30: 
  31: ## Authentication and Signing
  32: 
  33: ### Cookies
  34: - `sf_session`: session token (httpOnly)
  35: - `sf_workstation`: workstation binding token (httpOnly)
  36: 
  37: ### Station header
  38: - `x-station-token` is required by platform workflows and is attached to all frontend requests.
  39: 
  40: ### Signed request headers (authenticated protected routes)
  41: - `x-sf-timestamp` ISO timestamp
  42: - `x-sf-nonce` unique nonce
  43: - `x-sf-signature` HMAC SHA-256 hex digest
  44: 
  45: Payload format used for signature:
  46: `METHOD\nPATH_WITH_QUERY\nTIMESTAMP\nNONCE\nSHA256(JSON_BODY_OR_EMPTY)`
  47: 
  48: ### Unsigned routes
  49: - `/api/auth/bootstrap/status`
  50: - `/api/auth/restore`
  51: - `/api/auth/bootstrap/admin`
  52: - `/api/auth/login`
  53: - `/api/auth/pin/reenter`
  54: 
  55: All other authenticated application APIs require valid signed headers.

   1: # SentinelFit Design
   2: 
   3: ## Purpose
   4: SentinelFit is an on-prem operations platform for gyms and studios. It combines role-based operations (Member, Coach, Administrator), biometric governance, reporting, and recovery workflows in a Docker-first deployment.
   5: 
   6: ## Repository Layout
   7: - `frontend/`: Vue 3 + TypeScript SPA
   8: - `backend/`: Express + TypeScript API and background jobs
   9: - `tests/e2e/`: Playwright end-to-end coverage
  10: - `docker-compose.yml`: canonical runtime topology
  11: - `run_tests.sh`: canonical end-to-end verification script
  12: 
  13: ## Runtime Architecture
  14: - Frontend container serves the UI on port `5173`.
  15: - Backend container serves REST endpoints on port `3000`.
  16: - MySQL primary stores operational data.
  17: - MySQL standby is used for dry-run restore verification.
  18: - Shared folder path is mounted for report export distribution.
  19: 
  20: ### Startup sequence
  21: 1. Backend loads validated configuration.
  22: 2. Database initialization creates schema, applies compatibility column checks, and installs immutable audit triggers.
  23: 3. Key vault metadata is synchronized into `encryption_keys`.
  24: 4. Services are instantiated.
  25: 5. Session and artifact hardening migrations run.
  26: 6. Reporting schedules and ops background jobs are registered.
  27: 
  28: ## Core Design Principles
  29: - Backend is authoritative for access control, validation, and persistence.
  30: - Signed requests protect authenticated API routes against tampering and replay.
  31: - Workstation binding cookie is required for session continuity and PIN restore.
  32: - Sensitive fields are encrypted at rest using key-version metadata.
  33: - Audit integrity is enforced in MySQL with immutable triggers.
  34: - Dockerized behavior is considered the source of truth.
  35: 
  36: ## Security Model
  37: 
  38: ### Authentication and session lifecycle
  39: - Login is password-based with complexity requirements.
  40: - Optional PIN re-entry supports warm-lock restore on the same workstation.
  41: - Session timeout and account lockout policies are enforced server-side.
  42: - Warm-lock stores state in session and requires PIN restore if configured.
  43: 
  44: ### Request integrity
  45: Authenticated routes require:
  46: - `x-sf-timestamp`
  47: - `x-sf-nonce`
  48: - `x-sf-signature`
  49: - `x-station-token`
  50: 
  51: Signature payload format:
  52: `METHOD\nPATH\nTIMESTAMP\nNONCE\nSHA256(JSON_BODY)`
  53: 
  54: Controls:
  55: - timestamp freshness window
  56: - nonce replay protection
  57: - per-IP rate limits for sign-in and signed routes
  58: - workstation binding cookie verification
  59: 
  60: ### Network and access restrictions
  61: - IP allowlist middleware blocks requests outside configured ranges.
  62: - Role checks are enforced on route groups and domain operations.
  63: 
  64: ## Roles and Authorization
  65: - `Administrator`: full access, plus inherited `Coach` and `Member` capabilities.
  66: - `Coach`: coaching operations and assigned-location member/content scope.
  67: - `Member`: self profile, self consent, face workflow access to own records.
  68: 
  69: UI navigation is role-filtered and backend authorization is mandatory for data operations.
  70: 
  71: ## Data Model Summary
  72: Primary entities:
  73: - Identity: `users`, `user_roles`, `sessions`, `pin_credentials`, `failed_login_attempts`, `bootstrap_guard`
  74: - Security keys: `encryption_keys`
  75: - Member ops: `member_profiles`, `coach_assignments`, `coach_location_assignments`, `consent_records`
  76: - Biometrics: `face_records`, `face_record_versions`, `biometric_audit_log`
  77: - Content analytics: `content_posts`, `content_view_events`, `search_events`
  78: - Dashboards/reports: `dashboard_layouts`, `report_templates`, `report_schedules`, `report_subscriptions`, `report_exports`, `report_inbox_items`
  79: - Observability/recovery: `application_logs`, `access_logs`, `anomaly_alerts`, `backup_runs`, `recovery_dry_runs`, `maintenance_mode`
  80: 
  81: ### Integrity constraints
  82: - Biometric audit immutability is enforced with DB triggers that reject updates/deletes.
  83: - Face artifacts and session secrets are stored encrypted with key IDs.
  84: - Report subscriptions are unique per schedule/user.
  85: 
  86: ## Backend Module Responsibilities
  87: 
  88: ### Auth service
  89: - bootstrap-first-admin flow
  90: - password login and lockout tracking
  91: - session create/revoke/touch/warm-lock
  92: - PIN setup and re-entry with workstation binding validation
  93: 
  94: ### Member service
  95: - member creation with encrypted phone storage
  96: - coach assignment and coach-location management
  97: - consent recording and recipient listing
  98: - role-aware member visibility
  99: 
 100: ### Face service
 101: - timed liveness challenge issuance/consumption
 102: - server-side trusted metadata extraction
 103: - blur/liveness checks and dedup checks
 104: - enrollment versioning, deactivation, history, and audit retrieval
 105: 
 106: ### Content service
 107: - post publishing by role/location
 108: - view and search event capture with station attribution
 109: - filtered analytics with location authorization and date validation
 110: 
 111: ### Dashboard service
 112: - per-admin layout persistence
 113: - reusable template creation/listing
 114: 
 115: ### Report service
 116: - schedule creation and cron registration
 117: - ad-hoc report generation (CSV/Excel/PDF)
 118: - inbox delivery and download flow
 119: - shared-folder write with partial-failure alerting
 120: 
 121: ### Ops service
 122: - backup generation and retention cleanup
 123: - dry-run restore to standby MySQL with checksum validation
 124: - runtime metrics aggregation for admin console
 125: - retention and scheduled background maintenance jobs
 126: 
 127: ## Frontend Design
 128: - Single Vue SPA with module views for overview, members, faces, content, analytics, dashboards, reports, inbox, and admin console.
 129: - Pinia auth store manages session state, warm-lock state, and active view authorization.
 130: - API client signs authenticated requests and keeps unsigned auth bootstrap/login/restore routes separate.
 131: - Feature composables isolate domain logic:
 132:   - `useMembers`
 133:   - `useFaceOps`
 134:   - `useContentAnalytics`
 135:   - `useDashboardsReports`
 136:   - `useAdminConsole`
 137: 
 138: ## API and Contract Conventions
 139: - Success envelope:
 140:   - `{ "ok": true, "data": ... }`
 141: - Error envelope:
 142:   - `{ "ok": false, "error": { "code": string, "message": string, "details": object|null } }`
 143: - Validation is Zod-based per route.
 144: - Cookies:
 145:   - `sf_session` (httpOnly)
 146:   - `sf_workstation` (httpOnly)
 147: 
 148: ## Operational Workflows
 149: - Bootstrap (clean install): first admin creation when no admin exists.
 150: - Normal auth: login, signed session usage, optional warm-lock and PIN restore.
 151: - Member lifecycle: enrollment, consent, coach assignment, scoped listing.
 152: - Biometric lifecycle: challenge, dedup preview, enroll, history, deactivation, audit trail.
 153: - Reporting lifecycle: template, schedule/subscribers, export, inbox download.
 154: - Recovery lifecycle: backup creation and dry-run restore pass/fail verification.
 155: 
 156: ## Testing Strategy Implemented
 157: - Backend unit/integration-style tests for services, routes, config, crypto, DB, and security.
 158: - Frontend tests for UI logic/composables.
 159: - Playwright E2E for admin, coach, member, inbox, and bootstrap paths.
 160: - Unified script executes install, typecheck, tests, Docker startup, e2e, and clean-install bootstrap verification.
 161: 
 162: ## Non-Functional Characteristics
 163: - Offline/on-prem by design (no external API dependency).
 164: - Defensive defaults for local deployment with explicit environment overrides.
 165: - Structured logging with sensitive key redaction.
 166: - Explicit error codes/messages for operator-facing troubleshooting.

The docs already show a few static inconsistencies with the shipped code, so I’m validating whether tests compensate for that or whether core risks still go untested. I’m focusing on route/security tests first, then representative backend/frontend coverage.

   1: (1) HMAC Secret Storage
   2: 
   3: Question: How can we securely store HMAC-SHA256 secrets for request signing in a client-side environment without exposing them?
   4: 
   5: My Understanding: Each client needs a secret key to sign requests, but a static secret in the source code is insecure.
   6: 
   7: Solution: Implement a Session-Specific Secret generated by the server after a successful login handshake, stored only in memory (Pinia/Vuex) and rotated per session.
   8: 
   9: (2) Encryption Key Rotation
  10: 
  11: Question: How do we rotate keys every 180 days without the performance hit of re-encrypting the entire database?
  12: 
  13: My Understanding: Data encrypted with old keys must remain accessible after the rotation period.
  14: 
  15: Solution: Implement Key Versioning by storing a key_id alongside encrypted blobs and maintaining a "Key Vault" of historical keys to avoid massive batch re-encryption.
  16: 
  17: (3) Face Deduplication Scope
  18: 
  19: Question: Should the deduplication check against a local similarity threshold include inactive members?
  20: 
  21: My Understanding: We only need to check against current, active users to ensure uniqueness.
  22: 
  23: Solution: The check must include Deactivated accounts to prevent banned or former members from re-enrolling as new users to bypass fees or restrictions.
  24: 
  25: (4) PIN Re-entry vs. Session Expiry
  26: 
  27: Question: How does the optional PIN interact with the standard 30-minute session expiry?
  28: 
  29: My Understanding: The PIN is a convenience feature for "warm" workstations.
  30: 
  31: Solution: Maintain a Full Session timer (30 mins) and a separate PIN Lock Timer (5 mins) that triggers a UI overlay. If the full session expires, the PIN is invalidated and a password is required.
  32: 
  33: (5) Liveness Challenge Validation
  34: 
  35: Question: What constitutes a successful validation of the two-step head-turn challenge?
  36: 
  37: My Understanding: The system simply captures two frames to confirm movement.
  38: 
  39: Solution: The backend must validate that biometric landmarks (like nose tip and eye alignment) have shifted significantly and in the correct direction to prevent spoofing with a static photo.
  40: 
  41: (6) Offline Shared Folder Path
  42: 
  43: Question: How will the Express backend interface with the designated "offline" shared folder for reports?
  44: 
  45: My Understanding: The server saves files to a standard network drive.
  46: 
  47: Solution: Define a configurable SMB/UNC path mapping in the .env file and ensure the Express service account has explicit write permissions for that specific volume.
  48: 
  49: (7) Dashboard Builder Permissions
  50: 
  51: Question: How can we allow admins to use a drag-and-drop dashboard builder without overwriting each other's layouts?
  52: 
  53: My Understanding: Admins can globally move and configure widgets.
  54: 
  55: Solution: Implement a Layout Metadata Schema in MySQL that stores a JSON blob of coordinates and ChartIDs per user/role, allowing for personalized views.
  56: 
  57: (8) Face Capture Quality Gates
  58: 
  59: Question: Where should the "Quality Gate" feedback (blur, positioning) be processed to ensure immediate feedback?
  60: 
  61: My Understanding: The user needs to know if their photo is acceptable in real-time.
  62: 
  63: Solution: Process Initial Gates (blur, face-in-frame) in the browser via TensorFlow.js for speed, while the Final Gate (similarity/deduplication) is handled by the backend for security.
  64: 
  65: (9) Content View Attribution
  66: 
  67: Question: How do we track content views by source channel (e.g., Kiosk vs. Terminal) if the user isn't logged in?
  68: 
  69: My Understanding: We need to track which station the data is being viewed from.
  70: 
  71: Solution: Require the frontend to pass a DeviceID or StationToken in the analytics API headers to differentiate between various physical hardware locations.
  72: 
  73: (10) Audit Trail Immutability
  74: 
  75: Question: How do we guarantee the audit trail remains immutable for the required 7-year retention period?
  76: 
  77: My Understanding: Records are simply marked as "do not delete" in the application logic.
  78: 
  79: Solution: Implement a Database Trigger that explicitly blocks UPDATE and DELETE commands on the audit_logs table and triggers a system integrity alert if attempted.
  80: 
  81: (11) Role Hierarchy and Navigation
  82: 
  83: Question: How should the navigation and permissions be structured between Coaches and Admins?
  84: 
  85: My Understanding: Coaches see a subset of features compared to Admins.
  86: 
  87: Solution: Use Inclusive Permissions where higher roles inherit all permissions of lower roles, using a v-if directive to filter navigation links based on the user's hierarchy level.
  88: 
  89: (12) Deactivation vs. Search Trends
  90: 
  91: Question: How should deactivated member data affect analytics and search trend reports?
  92: 
  93: My Understanding: Deactivated members are simply flagged as inactive in the database.
  94: 
  95: Solution: Analytics queries must filter for active status by default to avoid skewing performance metrics; historical data should only be included via an explicit toggle.
  96: 
  97: (13) API Rate Limiting vs. Kiosks
  98: 
  99: Question: How do we prevent a 60 RPM limit from blocking multiple kiosks sharing a single LAN IP?
 100: 
 101: My Understanding: A standard IP-based rate limit will protect the server.
 102: 
 103: Solution: Implement Identity-Based Rate Limiting, allowing higher thresholds for authenticated stations and users while keeping the unauthenticated "Sign-in" page strictly capped.
 104: 
 105: (14) Subscription Delivery Failure
 106: 
 107: Question: What happens if a report subscription fails to deliver to the shared folder due to a network error?
 108: 
 109: My Understanding: The report is generated and the system tries again later.
 110: 
 111: Solution: Use a Background Worker (Cron) to deliver the report to an "In-App Inbox" regardless of folder availability, flagging a "Storage Sync Error" for admins to investigate.
 112: 
 113: (15) Restore Workflow Endpoint
 114: 
 115: Question: What is the intended target and validation process for the "Restore Workflow" endpoint?
 116: 
 117: My Understanding: The system needs a way to recover from data corruption or failure.
 118: 
 119: Solution: Target a Standby Recovery Instance using a "Dry Run" mode that validates checksums and decryption keys before performing any overwrite on the production MySQL schema.

*** Add File: .tmp/sentinelfit-static-audit-2026-04-06.md
+# SentinelFit Static Audit
+
+## 1. Verdict
+- Overall conclusion: Partial Pass
+
+## 2. Scope and Static Verification Boundary
+- Reviewed: repository documentation, manifests, backend routes/services/schema/config, frontend SPA shell/composables/components, and static test suites under `repo/backend/tests`, `repo/frontend/tests`, and `repo/tests/e2e`.
+- Not reviewed: runtime behavior against a live browser, live camera hardware, real Docker orchestration, live MySQL behavior, actual shared-folder permissions, or real cron execution.
+- Intentionally not executed: project startup, Docker, tests, Playwright, and any external services.
+- Manual verification required for: Docker startup health, real camera capture/import, actual drag-and-drop behavior in browser, real shared-folder export delivery, real backup/restore against MySQL standby, and runtime rendering/performance.
+
+## 3. Repository / Requirement Mapping Summary
+- Core business goal mapped: offline/on-prem gym operations platform covering password sign-in with optional same-workstation PIN re-entry, RBAC (`Member`/`Coach`/`Administrator`), member enrollment and coach assignment, face consent/capture/dedup/governance, coaching content analytics, admin dashboard/reporting, and offline inbox/shared-folder report delivery.
+- Main implementation areas mapped: Express backend (`repo/backend/src/routes`, `repo/backend/src/services`, `repo/backend/src/schema.ts`), Vue SPA (`repo/frontend/src/App.vue`, composables, components), operational docs (`repo/README.md`, `docs/*.md`), and static tests (`repo/backend/tests`, `repo/frontend/tests`, `repo/tests/e2e`).
+
+## 4. Section-by-section Review
+
+### 1. Hard Gates
+
+#### 1.1 Documentation and static verifiability
+- Conclusion: Partial Pass
+- Rationale: The repo has substantial documentation and manifests, but reviewer-facing docs are internally inconsistent and reference missing/out-of-scope artifacts, which weakens static verification.
+- Evidence: `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\README.md:5`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\README.md:31`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\README.md:34`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\docs\api-spec.md:48`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\docs\api-spec.md:85`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\docs\api-spec.md:165`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\docs\design.md:130`
+- Manual verification note: None beyond correcting the documentation drift.
+

+- Conclusion: Partial Pass
+- Rationale: The implementation is clearly centered on the prompt, but one important offline multi-station constraint is weakened by IP-only throttling, and the default admin/coach overview path is miswired through a member-only self-profile flow.
+- Evidence: `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\middleware.ts:78`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\middleware.ts:88`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\middleware.ts:127`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\frontend\src\App.vue:177`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\frontend\src\composables\useMembers.ts:52`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\routes\self.ts:13`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\services\member-service.ts:100`
+
+### 2. Delivery Completeness
+
+#### 2.1 Core requirement coverage
+- Conclusion: Partial Pass
+- Rationale: Most explicit prompt requirements are implemented statically across backend and frontend: auth/PIN, RBAC, member enrollment, face consent/capture/dedup/history, content analytics, dashboard builder, report scheduling/export/inbox/shared-folder path, and admin console. The most material gap found is the default overview flow for coach/admin users, which routes through member-only profile logic and silently fails.
+- Evidence: `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\routes\auth.ts:46`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\routes\members.ts:31`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\routes\faces.ts:28`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\routes\content.ts:89`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\routes\dashboards.ts:27`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\routes\reports.ts:29`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\routes\admin.ts:13`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\frontend\src\App.vue:119`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\frontend\src\App.vue:177`
+
+#### 2.2 Basic end-to-end deliverable vs partial/demo
+- Conclusion: Pass
+- Rationale: The repo contains a full project structure with frontend, backend, schema, ops docs, test suites, and e2e specifications rather than isolated example code.
+- Evidence: `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\README.md:29`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\app.ts:36`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\frontend\src\App.vue:1`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\tests\e2e\app.spec.ts:72`
+
+### 3. Engineering and Architecture Quality

+#### 3.1 Structure and module decomposition
+- Conclusion: Pass
+- Rationale: Responsibilities are separated reasonably across routes, services, composables, and components for the problem size; there is no evidence of a single-file pileup.
+- Evidence: `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\app.ts:8`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\docs\design.md:86`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\frontend\src\App.vue:3`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\docs\design.md:127`

+
+#### 4.2 Real product/service vs demo-only
+- Conclusion: Pass
+- Rationale: The codebase resembles a real product shape, including schema, RBAC, reporting/export paths, backup flows, and dedicated tests.
+- Evidence: `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\schema.ts:1`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\services\report-service.ts:47`, `C:\Hale\Eaglepoint\Task-75\repo\backend\src\services\ops-service.ts:86`
+
+### 5. Prompt Understanding and Requirement Fit
